August 26, 2024
Greetings colleagues, partners and fellow Houstonians,
The Houston Health Department created this “status of community health” data report to
inform its partners and stakeholders. The report serves as the department’s guiding document,
offering us recommendations on our community health improvement plan and our strategic
plan. These items steer our path forward to address population health in our region.
The department created this report using primary and secondary data while keeping health
equity front and center. We recognize that many of the health challenges in our region, and
in our country, are a result of structural and systemic influences. We know that addressing
these issues can only be done if we all come together. In partnership with you, we will
continually strive to improve health outcomes for Houstonians - now and in the future.
We are grateful for our remarkable partners and hope that you find this report useful.
With best regards,
Stephen L. Williams, M.Ed., MPA
Director Houston Health Department

II. Background
In 2004, upon the recommendation from the Institutes of Medicine, the Centers for Disease Control and
Prevention identified accreditation as a critical strategy for the strengthening of public health agencies’
infrastructure and performance. After several revisions, the the Public Health Accreditation Board was created,
with a list of standards. The accreditation process of the National Public Health Departments was launched on
September 14, 2011. The accreditation process ensures that health departments comply with the standardized
requirements to improve their ability to address community health problems efficiently. The first step is to
evaluate the health needs of the community. This first step, the Community Health Assessment, is one of the
standardized requirements for accreditation and stands as a critical component that complements the rest.
The Community Health Assessment (CHA) has long been used by public health departments, hospitals and
other social service agencies. It is a systematic process carried out by health organizations to obtain specific
information on target communities. The results of CHA inform programmatic decisions, as well as strategic
orientations for systems and services; and it is also a way to explore the progress made by existing programs.
CHA is one of the components of “Assessment,” which is one of the three core functions of public health.
Artist: Royal Sumikat • 707 Henderson Street
In 2014, the Houston Health Department (HHD) received accreditation from the Public Health Accreditation
Bureau (PHAB). HHD was the first accredited health department in Texas and the second large city health
department to receive accreditation in the U.S. Accredited departments are expected to evolve, improving and
advancing to maintain the best possible conditions, thus becoming increasingly efficient in addressing the
health problems of the population served. Accredited departments must apply for re-accreditation to maintain
the status of the accreditation (PHAB, 2024). HHD received re-accrediation in 2020 and is continuing to make
progress towards its goals. This CHA will replace the prior CHA (2018-2022).

IV. Findings
Demographics of Houston
Houston is the fourth largest city in the US, with
an estimated 2021 population of 2.3 million.
Most of Houston is contained within Harris
County. Harris County, including Houston, is
home to approximately 4.7 million residents
and has been growing rapidly, adding 1.3
million residents over the last 20 years. The
Houston metropolitan area, sometimes
referred to as Greater Houston, encompasses
a nine-county area of Harris and surrounding
counties that stretches to Galveston and
along the Gulf Coast. This area, also known
as the Houston-The Woodlands-Sugar Land
Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA), contains
approximately 7.3 million residents, according
to U.S. Census estimates. This area has been
growing rapidly for years.

Community Assets
Community assets: Resources that can enhance wellbeing
The Center for Community Health and Development defines community assets as, “anything that can be used
to improve the quality of community life” (Center for Community Health and Development at the University of
Kansas, n.d.). This includes physical resources such as parks, recreation centers, hospitals and schools, as well as
community services like food assistance programs and public transportation.
As the fourth most populous city in the United States (City of Houston, 2018), Houston boasts a variety of
community assets that residents can access to help meet their needs and improve their quality of life. However,
these assets are not uniformly distributed across the city (see Figure 1 for an example); many residents may
need assistance to find and access them. To address this, several Houston-area government and non-profit
organizations have created interactive online maps to help residents locate and make use of these resources.
Figure 1. Arts, Entertainment & Recreational Facilities, County by ZIP Code*
Data Source: InfoUSA Business Data (2020)
The City of Houston’s online map of neighborhood services, shown in Figure 2, is publicly available and includes
the following service and resource types: police & fire stations, hospitals, workforce solutions career offices,
libraries, schools & after school programs, community centers, museums, parks, and the Houston Health
Department’s multi-service centers. The map is designed to zoom into specific parts of the city, for a close-up
view of neighborhoods (City of Houston GIS, 2024a).

Access to safe and affordable housing is essential to wellbeing. To assist residents in locating affordable rental
housing, the City of Houston Housing and Community Development Department created an online resource
(Figure 4) that allows residents to enter their income, family size and desired ZIP Code and matches them with
rental properties that may meet their needs (Houston Housing and Community Development Department,
2022). For first-time homebuyers at lower income levels, the department also provides resources that can help
to offset down-payment costs (Houston Housing and Community Development Department, n.d.).
Figure 4. Application Locating Affordable Rental Housing
Source: https://houstontx.gov/housing/research.html
For those in need of food assistance, the non-profit organization Houston Food Bank distributes food through
a network of partner agencies. Residents can visit the “Find Help,” map application to locate food distribution
centers at https://www.houstonfoodbank.org/find-help/agency-locator (Houston Food Bank, n.d.).
A broad range of community assets and resources available to Houston area residents have been catalogued
by Family Houston, a United Way agency. Their listings include services available to seniors, financial assistance
programs, and help for survivors of domestic violence (Family Houston, n.d.). For more information, visit the
community resource website at www.familyhouston.org/community-resources-2.

Income Inequality
According to the U.S. Census Bureau,
income inequality, which refers to a
significant disparity in the distribution of
income, has increased in the U.S. for the

among those with lower incomes
(Semega & Kollar, 2022). Income
inequality is measured by the Gini Index,
which has a range of 0 to 1. A value of 0

among households and 1 indicates that
income is concentrated in a smaller

Public Health Actions
According to a 2014 study by the Haas Institute for a Fair and Inclusive Society at UC Berkeley, there are
six solutions to help economic inequality:
1.               Increase the minimum wage
2.               Expand the earned income tax
3.               Build assets for working families
4.               Invest in education
5.               Make the tax code more progressive
6.               End residential segregation (Powell, 2014)

Education
Many studies support the argument that basic educational expertise and skills, including fundamental
knowledge, reasoning ability, emotional self-regulation, and interactional abilities, are critical components of
health. Moreover, education is a fundamental social determinant of health, an upstream cause that contributes
to health. In the U.S., the improvement in health outcomes seen in greater educational attainment has
steepened over decades, producing a larger gap in health status between Americans with high education
compared to those with less education.
The Texas Education Agency (TEA) produces a variety of resources related to graduates and dropouts in Texas
public schools, including data and information on high school graduation and dropout rates.
The following charts compare four-year high school graduation rates and dropout rates between Houston
Independent School District (HISD) and other Texas public secondary schools. HISD has lower graduation rates
and higher dropout rates compared to Texas. The dropout rate for HISD decreased from 13.6% for the Class of
2019 to 10.5% for the Class of 2021 (Texas Education Agency, 2022).

• Collaborate with health practitioners, educators, schools, and communities to implement educational

Living Conditions
Neighborhood Concerns
In a multi-faceted and dynamic city like Houston, it is expected that residents will have various neighborhood
concerns. To understand these concerns, HHD asked the Houstonians as part of the Community Health Survey
(2024), “What are the top three concerns related to living conditions in your neighborhood?” The responses
were very diverse, but some of the key themes are as below.
Crime/Violence
Like any major city, Houston has areas that experience higher crime rates. Residents are concerned about
personal safety, property crimes, and the effectiveness of law enforcement in their neighborhoods. There is
a growing call for increased police presence, community policing initiatives, and crime prevention programs.

DPS data is divided into two categories: violent crime (murder, rape, robbery, and aggravated assault) and

comparisons of Houston, Texas, and the U.S. in violent crime; and Houston and Texas in non-violent crime.
Houston shows the highest crime rate in both charts.
Public Health Actions
1. Build resilience and reduce susceptibility to crime.
2. Educate the public to build healthy gender norms.
3. Encourage residents to develop healthy relationships.
4. Create protective environments.
5. Reform the juvenile and criminal justice systems to keep youth and young adults on track.
6. Foster neighborhood-oriented law enforcement strategies.

Noise
Noise is another concern in many Houston neighborhoods. Uncomfortable noise levels can result from:
• Traffic from highways, major roads, and intersections, especially during peak hours.
• Construction projects for buildings, road expansions, and infrastructure improvements.
• Airport aircraft noise from takeoffs, landings, and flyovers.
• Houston’s entertainment scene,
including music venues, clubs,          Noise level generated by air, road and rail in the Houston area.
and bars.
• Commercial and industrial
activities, such as factories,
warehouses, or delivery trucks.
• Events and festivals throughout
the year, with noise levels and
increased traffic.
The map shows that the north
side of the City of Houston where
George Bush International airport
is located and the northeast side
of Houston along the 45 corridor
and Ellington airfield area have
increased levels of noise. High noise
levels are also seen in the southeast.
Graffiti
Graffiti issues can be a neighborhood concern. Illegal or unauthorized graffiti involves defacing public or
private property without permission. It can include the use of spray paint, markers, stickers, or other materials
to create markings, tags, or images. Illegal graffiti is often seen as vandalism and can negatively impact the
aesthetics, property values, and sense of safety in a neighborhood. Some graffiti in Houston may be associated
with gang activity or territorial markings. Gang-related graffiti can raise concerns about public safety, criminal
activity, and the perception of the neighborhood.
Public Health Actions
• Support a 3-1-1 helpline for residents to report graffiti sightings.
• Support programs to remove graffiti from public property.
• Support ordinances that prohibit illegal graffiti and impose penalties on offenders.
• Collaborate with neighborhood associations, community organizations, and concerned residents to
develop graffiti prevention and removal strategies, such as organizing volunteer clean-up events or
reporting graffiti hotspots to ensure timely removal.
• Create designated spaces and opportunities for legal art murals and public art projects to channel
creative energy into positive and visually appealing forms of street art.

Air Quality
National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) were established under the Clean Air Act (U.S. EPA, 2014).
NAAQS classified six pollutants as threats to human health: sulfur dioxide, lead, carbon monoxide, nitrogen
dioxide, particulate matter, and ozone. Ground level ozone is formed in the presence of sunlight when volatile
organic compounds (VOC) and oxides of nitrogen (NOx) react in the air. NOx and VOCs are emitted into the
ambient air by car emissions, chemical plants, refineries, power plants, and more (U.S. EPA, 2015a). Particulate
matter (PM) enters the air predominantly from power plants, industrial emissions, and motor vehicles (U.S.
Ozone and particulate matter both affect human health. When these are elevated in the air, they can cause
or exacerbate respiratory, heart and cardiovascular illnesses (U.S. EPA, 2015b, 2016b). Currently the Texas
Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ) lists the Houston-Galveston-Brazoria area as in moderate non-
attainment for ozone according to the 2015 standard and severe non-attainment for the 2008 standard for the
8-hr average of ozone (Texas Commission on Environmental Quality, 2024). The American Lung Association’s
“State of Air” report ranked Houston 8th in the nation for most ozone pollution and as a city with poor air quality
in regard to ozone and particulate matter (American Lung Association, n.d.).

Housing

Census Bureau, n.d.)n.d.. That is an increase of 9.8% over that decade and highlights the need for additional
housing for the city.
Housing shortages are a challenge facing the nation, worsening since the housing market collapse of 2008.
According to Fannie Mae, the decade following the recession saw the least amount of new homes built since
the 1960s, driving up housing costs as available housing became limited (Hayward, 2022). More recently,

Gentrification and Displacement
The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) defines gentrification as “the transformation of
neighborhoods from low value to high value [with] the potential to cause displacement of long-time residents
and businesses” (CDC, 2009). In their Guide to Measuring Neighborhood Change to Understand and Prevent
Displacement, the National Neighborhood Indicators Partnership (NNIP) states that gentrification is the
“transformation of areas historically inhabited by marginalized groups, usually racial or ethnic or class groups,
into areas used by the dominant class or racial or ethnic group. Usually characterized by increased investments
Public Health Actions
• Encourage landlords to accept HUD Housing Choice Vouchers to increase available low-income
housing (Harvard Joint Center for Housing Studies, 2022).
•    Collaborate with local leaders and groups to encourage affordable housing development, especially
in areas that have lower risk for severe natural disaster damage.
•    Work with local disaster response groups to ensure that rental assistance following disasters is
provided to households in a timely manner.
in areas that have seen long-term disinvestment” (Cohen & Pettit, 2019). Both of these definitions can be used
to describe several inner-city Houston neighborhoods that have seen marked increases in median housing
costs, income and education levels, and a large influx of residents that are White non-Hispanic, during a time
(2000 to 2021) when that population declined across Houston as a whole.
In 2016, the City of Los Angeles published the Los Angeles Index of Neighborhood Change (City of Los
Angeles, 2016) to demonstrate ZIP Code level demographic shifts that took place over the preceding years.
This gentrification index combines measures of economic and demographic change. The Houston Health
Department has adapted the Los Angeles method to describe gentrification in Houston. Using ZIP Code level
data from the 2000 Decennial Census and the 2021 American Community Survey, the Houston Gentrification
Index includes changes in the following measures over the last two decades:

Public Health Actions
• Educate the community to raise awareness of the negative effects of gentrification.
• Collaborate with community-based organizations to assist long-term residents with information about
how to protect their neighborhoods.
• Support housing policy reform to protect established communities (Hayes, 2022).
• Encourage utilization of “connected urban neighborhoods” that create clusters of shops, restaurants,
and stores in walkable distances, with store owners offered rents based on the volume of business so
they can pass along affordable goods and services (Fulton, 2022).
• As residents are displaced by rising costs, encourage existing support services such as health centers

Transportation
Transportation is a necessary function throughout the world. In the local area, motor vehicles provide much of
that function. While necessary, motor vehicles also contribute to public health concerns, in particular vehicle
crashes and emissions. In 2021 there were 1.56 deaths per hundred million vehicle miles traveled in Texas, for a
total of 4,497 fatalities. This means one person was killed in a motor vehicle accident every 1 hour and 57 minutes
in the state. Of these fatalities, 433 were involved in distracted driving accidents. Over a quarter of a million people
were injured in a motor vehicle crashes in 2021, at 239,539 persons (Texas Department of Transportation, 2021).
Beside vehicle crashes, emissions from motor vehicles also impact human health. Ozone is created by the
combination of nitrogen oxides and volatile organic compounds (VOC) mix in the presence of sunlight, both
of which are emitted by motor vehicles. Another emission from motor vehicles is particulate matter. These
emissions affect respiratory and cardiovascular health (U.S. EPA, n.d.-a).
As of 2021, almost ¾ of Houston’s workers (73.7%), drove alone
in a car or truck to their place of employment. The number and
percentage of workers driving to work alone has declined from
five years ago in 2017, when 76.6% of workers drove alone to
work. This trend is also seen in Texas and the U.S. The decline
in Houstonians driving a car or truck alone to work is unlikely
to be attributed to more workers carpooling or using public
transportation – both carpooling and public transportation use
decreased from 2017 to 2021. The most likely source of fewer
workers driving alone to work in Houston, Texas, and nationally
is the increase in employees working from home. In 2017, only
39,704 (3.6%) people worked from home in Houston. This more
than doubled over the next five years at an increase of 127.4% in 2021. The number of people working from
home in 2021 increased to 90,607 (8.3%). This trend was moderately increasing prior to 2020, but noticeably
increased during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Very few workers walk or bike to work in Houston. As of 2021, less than half a percent biked and only 1.9%
walked to work in Houston.
Public Health Actions
• Partner with other sectors, such as urban planning and public safety, to reduce traffic congestion and
accidents, increase public transport options, and improve electric vehicle accessibility.
• Coordinate with community-based organizations to provide education for residents about the use of
public transportation and car pools.

Exposure to Toxins
Environmental toxins can pose a risk to human health, especially after long periods of exposure. Humans are
exposed to these in various means, such as through their occupation or from the air, water, or soil around their
home. Toxins often come from past and current industrial operations. Other sources are household products
such as plastics, Styrofoam, electronic equipment, and cleaning products.
Lead
Lead was banned for use in household paint in 1978, but can still be found in older homes. Other sources of
lead exposure include soil, toys, food storage, dust, cosmetics, and folk remedies (U.S. EPA, n.d.-b), and pottery
from Mexico and some Asian countries (California Dept. of Public Health, n.d.).
According to the CDC, Lead exposure is most harmful in children less than six years old. According to the CDC
lead exposure in children can cause damage to the brain and nervous system, slow growth and development,
learning and behavior problems, and speech and hearing problems (CDC, 2022). As of 2020, 12.6% of
households in Houston have potential lead exposure. This can be compared to 8.7% of Texas households and
17.7% in the U.S. (City Health Dashboard, n.d.).
Nitrogen oxides
Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) is an outdoor and indoor air quality concern. Indoor nitrogen dioxide predominantly
comes from gas stoves and fireplaces. Nitrogen dioxide is a respiratory irritant and can be problematic for
those with asthma or other respiratory illnesses (U.S. EPA, n.d.-c). The American Housing Survey by the U.S.
Census Bureau found 64% of Houston households use gas in their homes.
Benzene/Butadiene
Sources for benzene and 1,3-butadiene are from manufacturing processes, motor vehicle exhaust, and
cigarette smoke. Benzene is also found in gasoline, oil and coal burning, and industrial solvents (U.S. EPA,
2012). Butadiene is often found in rubber and plastic manufacturing (U.S. EPA, 2009). Both substances can be
very toxic to humans, causing increased susceptibility to cancers and damaging many body systems (U.S. EPA,
Since benzene and butadiene are so dangerous to humans, the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality
(TCEQ) has set levels that have the potential to cause harm to health and warrant further investigation.
Monitoring equipment is in place to measure the levels of benzene and butadiene in the air, predominantly in
southeast Houston, near the Houston Ship channel and areas with many industrial sites. The monitors measure
both short-term and long-term values. For benzene, the upper limits of safety are:
•   Short-term AMCV (Air Monitoring Comparison Value) = 180 ppb
•   Long-term AMCV = 1.4 ppb

Public Health Actions
• Monitor the presence of toxins in the Houston area.
• Partner with community-based organizations to provide education for neighborhoods about local
toxins and how to avoid exposure.
• Educate parents of young children who are most likely to be exposed to lead about preventing
contact with lead and how they can have their children under age six tested annually for lead.
• Educate residents, especially those in the Asian and Hispanic communities, about lead in pottery and
dishes (California Department of Public Health, n.d.).
• Support programs for lead hazard identification, stabilization, or removal for low-income homes.
• Educate residents about the dangers of gas stoves and the importance of removing the stoves or
providing additional ventilation.

Disasters, Emergency Preparedness and Response
Flooding
Extreme weather has led to most of the local disasters requiring emergency response. Houston’s flat topography,
subsidence, network of creeks, bayous, lakes and reservoirs, and proximity to the Gulf Coast make the city
susceptible to watershed flooding, areal flooding from heavy rainfall, and severe tropical weather. The city is
also prone to extreme heat and cold events and the occasional tornado. The effect of climate change is expected
to increase the severity and frequency of each of these natural threats.
The Harris County Flood Control District (HCFCD) is the local
agency responsible for flood management and modeling.               100-year Floodplain and At-Risk Population
in Harris County
HCFCD’s most recent 100-yr floodplain map shows many
people are at a high risk in the next flood event. The most at-
risk population for a 100-year flood event are in some of
Houston’s most vulnerable populations. Southwest Houston
has the highest population density at-risk for flooding, as
shown on the map. These are the Alief and Sharpstown
neighborhood areas located in the Braes Bayou watershed.
The northern location with the highest density is in the
Greenspoint neighborhood, and the central location is
Downtown Houston. There are many other pockets in the
county where floods would impact thousands of lower-
income people in neighborhoods such as Acres Homes,

While the HCFCD has updated their 100-yr flood plain maps in 2021, only relying on this information to assess
flood risk is shortsighted. For instance, in the decade between 1999-2009 most of the flooded areas were
outside of a 100-year flood plain. This same proportion of flood damage also occurred during Hurricane Harvey
in 2017. According to FEMA’s flood model there are over 100,000 (13%) of residential properties that are in
the 500-year floodplain (Understanding Houston, n.d.). Calls to 311 in Houston reporting flooding or drainage
issues are scattered across the entire area of Houston, indicating that flooding and drainage problems are not
only in designated floodplain areas. HCFCD advises that every home in Houston should have flood insurance.
Flooding disproportionately impacts low-income and non-white people. (National Academies of Sciences,
Engineering, and Medicine, 2019). This is especially true in Houston where these groups have historically
lived on low-lying land (a result of decades of discriminatory housing policies), receive fewer flood mitigation
projects, and are characterized by decades of disinvestment, such as poor stormwater infrastructure
(Chakraborty, et al., 2019).
Major storms often bring widespread loss of power and municipal water, and natural gas supply disruptions.
Especially vulnerable are those who rely on electricity to operate life-sustaining devices, or have other medical
dependencies such as hemodialysis, home health, home hospice, or oxygen services.
Extreme Heat and Cold
Other weather extremes like heat and hard freezes can also displace populations with the need for cooling or
warming centers. Both hot and cold temperature extremes can trigger power loss. Additionally, freeze events
can cause pipes to burst and cause extensive home damage.
When extreme weather impacts are compounded with sustained power disruptions the consequences can be
dire. Winter Storm Uri in February 2021 had widespread power outages across the state. Uri has been implicated
for 246 Texas deaths and $80-130 billion in damages according to the Department of State Health Services
(DSHS) and the Texas Comptroller, respectively.
Houston Health Department                                                                      2024 Houston Community Health Assessment
Pandemics
Pandemics like COVID-19 and other infectious disease outbreaks pose longer duration public health emergencies
than most other natural hazards and threats. The national disaster declaration for COVID-19 was in effect from
March 13, 2020 through May 11, 2024. The confirmed Houston case count is just over 548,000 and there have
been 4,885 COVID-19 deaths.
Technological and man-made emergencies such as refinery and tanker explosions, fires, and related toxic
releases are risks in Houston. Other types of man-made emergency threats to Houston could involve acts of
terrorism or bioterrorism.
New York City reported total Covid-19 cases instead of confirmed cases from May 2023.
Social Vulnerability
Regardless of the type of emergency or disaster, there are always groups who bear a disproportionate burden
of the impacts. These vulnerable population groups can include people with disabilities, those who need
help with accessing food and other basic needs, persons who need help with the activities of daily living,
the elderly, the young, the LGBTQIA+ community, people who are non-English speakers, people who lack
resources such as health insurance, and people who are otherwise underrepresented or are disenfranchised.
These people are priorities for public health in providing a safety net for those facing health inequities. The
Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) consolidates 16 different variables in four broad factor categories (socioeconomic
status, household characteristics, racial and ethnic minority status, housing type and transportation) to a single
index on vulnerability. The darkest colors on the map show the areas of highest social vulnerability. The circles
show Public Health Preparedness outreach and training events.
The SVI mapping for Houston generates a classic inverted “C” pattern that is also consistent with other specific
vulnerability mappings.
Houstonians need to develop emergency preparedness knowledge, skills and abilities to appropriately: (1)
shelter-in-place; (2) evacuate and shelter remotely; and (3) implement other necessary protective or control
measures. Challenges to overcome include access and affordability of health and property insurance, including
flood and windstorm policies; health literacy; and home back-up power options. Barriers include money,
competing needs, and complacency.

Public Health Actions:
• Educate the public about the emergency hazards and threats that may affect Houston, and how to
prepare and respond.
• Partner with national organizations such as the Red Cross and community organizations such as local
churches to provide shelters for those displaced by disasters.
• Coordinate many City of Houston emergency response efforts.
• Provide guidance to the community in times of disaster, such as the importance of vaccines and masks
during pandemics, and which communities need to evacuate in case of hurricanes.
• Provide needed care, such as disease testing and vaccinations during pandemics, and coordinate
services with local businesses, health care organizations, and other local groups.
• Provide warming and cooling centers and other resources in times of extreme cold or heat.
• Connect residents with helpful resources in preparing for and responding to emergency situations.

The Substance Use Prevention Program at Harris County Public Health reported that the primary cause of
increased overdose deaths appears to be the increase of fentanyl in the illegal drug supply, which is often

morphine and just 2mg can result in a lethal overdose. Fentanyl-involved overdose deaths in Harris County
increased 331% from 2019 to 2021 (Harris County Public Health, n.d.).

The Houston Health Department (2024) reported 1,232 opioid-related hospital visits in 2024 in Houston, which

be related to Fentanyl and 941 had an incident of Naloxone usage. In comparison to the 2022 annual report,
there was a 24.37% increase in the number of fentanyl visits and a 9.34% decrease in the number of visits with
naloxone usage. The top 10 ZIP Codes with the most opioid-related hospital visits were 77026, 77002, 77074,
77016, 77033, 77030, 77093, 77095 and 77004 (not in any order). The leading demographic of overdoses was
white males between the ages of 18-44.

Emergency Medical Services (EMS) transport data are also in line with the hospitalization data indicating
that certain ZIP Codes have higher numbers of EMS transports related to opioid overdose as well as a higher
burden of hospitalization due to opioid overdose. (Houston Health Department, 2024). Texas Department of
State Health Services data indicates that opioid-related emergency department visits have increased every
year in Harris County from 2016 (22.3 per 100,000 population) to 2021 (32.6 per 100, 000 population) (Texas
Health Data Dashboard, n.d.), but have stabilized recently.

Physical Activity
Physical activity has many health benefits, such as reducing risk of chronic diseases, including diabetes and
obesity; improving sleep; reducing blood pressure; and decreasing anxiety (CDC, 2024a).
“Reduce the proportion of adults who do no physical activity in their free time” is a goal of the U.S. Department
of Health and Human Services’ Healthy People 2030. For adults with no physical activity in their free time,
the national target is 21.8% or less. In 2021, 23.7% of adults in the U.S. were not physically active in their free
time, compared to Texas at 25.1%, and Houston at 22.4%. (CDC BRFSS, 2021).
In 2021, a sample of Harris County adults responded to questions in the Texas Department of State Health
Services BRFSS survey. One question was, “During the past month, other than your regular job, did you
participate in any physical activities or exercises, such as running, calisthenics, golf, gardening, or walking
for exercise?” A total of 21.7% replied “No” to this question. Of this group, 18.1% were male and 25.5% were
female. The following charts show that Whites were most likely to participate in leisure-time physical activity,
followed by Blacks and then Hispanics. Those with more education were also more likely to be physically
active during times that they were not working.

Data Source: TDSHS BRFSS (2021)
The Centers for Disease Control recommends 150 minutes of moderate exercise per week. However, according
to the Kinder Institute 2024 Houston Area Survey Report (Kinder Institute, 2024), around 3 in 10 Houstonians
reported getting 30 minutes or less of moderate exercise each week, with Houston residents reporting an
overall median (mid-point) activity level of 90 minutes of moderate exercise each week. This result indicates
that more than half of Houston residents do not meet the recommended guideline of 150 minutes of moderate
exercise per week.
Public Health Actions:
• Provide public education about physical exercise guidelines and how exercise improves health.
• Support policies that would increase public areas used for physical activity, such as parks, walking trails,
and roadway lanes for cyclists.
• Partner with community-based organizations to encourage physical activity in their areas.
• Encourage transportation that also involves physical activity, such as walking or biking to work or
school. For example, the Safe Route to Schools (SRTS) programs work to improve the ability of kids to
walk and bike to school in a safe and convenient manner (U.S. Department of Transportation, 2015).

Tobacco use in the U.S. has significantly decreased over time, dropping from 20.9% of adults in 2005 to 11.5%
in 2021. However, tobacco continues to be the number one cause of preventable illness, disability, and death
in the U.S., accounting for more than 480,000 deaths every year, or about 1 in 5 deaths (CDC, 2024b).

Texas laws have a large impact on tobacco and e-cigarette use, especially among young people. On June
7, 2019, the Texas governor signed SB 21, or Tobacco 21, which raised the minimum legal age in Texas to
purchase tobacco from 18 years to 21 years. Presently, 95% of smokers start before the age 21. Increasing the
age to purchase tobacco helps decrease access to tobacco for those under 21 and thus reduce the numbers of
persons under 21 who start to smoke. A National Academy of Medicine report found that among those aged
15 to 17 years, there was an approximate 25% decrease in initiation rates of tobacco use with an increase in
the tobacco sales age to 21 years (Paso Del Norte Health Foundation, 2019).
However, Texas has received a Grade ‘F’ on implementation of Tobacco 21 policy with a rank of 29 out of 50
states by the Preventing Tobacco Addiction Foundation (2020). The Foundation has evaluated the policies
of 44 states that have passed Tobacco 21 laws, raising the minimum legal age to purchase tobacco to age
21. The Foundation graded Texas law based on: a) tobacco retail licenses only cover cigarettes and not
e-cigarettes, b) the vaping rate among high school seniors is still high (18.7%), c) Texas spends $3.7M in
tobacco prevention efforts and the tobacco industry spends $618M in marketing. For every $1 spent on
prevention, Big Tobacco spends $167 in marketing, d) there is noe-cigarette tax, and e) the tax per pack of
cigarettes is $1.41. For comparison, New York state, graded ‘A’, has a $4.35 tax per pack of cigarettes and has
$0.80 tax on e-cigarettes. Also, Texas law has preemption included, to prevent local areas from raising the sales

• Inform, educate and empower the public about the risks of smoking and use of vaping products.
• Partner with community-based organizations to encourage smoking cessation and healthy life styles
in the community.

• Support limitations on who can purchase tobacco products and requirements of health warnings
on tobacco and vaping products.

• Enforce no-smoking ordinances.
• Monitor smoking and vaping trends and provide current and accurate data to the public
and policy-makers.

Health Literacy
The ability to find, comprehend, and use health information and services to make educated decisions about
one’s health is referred to as health literacy. Understanding health information, navigating the healthcare
system, and making educated decisions about one’s health requires a variety of skills, including the ability to
read, write, communicate, and apply math. People with low health literacy may struggle to understand health
information given to them by healthcare professionals, may have trouble navigating the healthcare system,
and may find it difficult to follow instructions from doctors or to make wise decisions regarding their health.
Reading and writing skills are among the core components of health literacy (Liu, Wang, Liu, et al., 2020).
Houston’s Adult Literacy Blueprint (Mayor’s Office, 2021) reported that 1 in every 3 adults in Harris County is
functionally illiterate. This means that more than 1 million adults in Harris County lack the necessary skills to
participate and engage fully in the workplace and society.
Low levels of literacy are highest among the Black and Hispanic populations, those with low incomes, and
those not born in the U.S. (Ballard Brief, 2017). Low health literacy is associated with poor health outcomes,
including higher rates of chronic diseases, hospitalization, and mortality. It can also contribute to health
disparities and inequities, as those with low health literacy may have limited access to health information and
resources (Hickey, et al., 2018).
Public Health Actions:
• Partner with community-based organizations and healthcare providers to support health literacy and
provide clear and understandable health information in multiple languages and with sensitivity to
clients’ cultural backgrounds to increase acceptance and understanding of health issues.
• Make healthcare information available in a variety of media, such as films, infographics, and pamphlets,
to help ensure that the messages will be understood. Provide translation services when needed.
• Improve communication between healthcare providers and patients through training in
communication skills and cultural awareness education for providers.
• Promote education and training on health literacy skills.
• Advocate for policies and systems changes that support health literacy, such as improving health
insurance coverage and reducing barriers to healthcare access.
Health Screenings
Regular screenings can aid in the early identification of potential health conditions, when treatment is
frequently more successful and minimally invasive. Many screenings are done as a part of routine physicals
or other primary care appointments. Some examples are checks for blood pressure, cholesterol and diabetes.
Many other screenings are designed to identify cancers in early stages, before symptoms are apparent.
Cancers found in the early stages are generally easier to treat and have a higher possibility of cure. It is crucial
to consult a healthcare professional about recommended screenings and to adhere to the suggested best
practices. Age, sex, and risk factors can all affect screening recommendations, which can also change over
time as more data becomes available.
Screenings for cancer are highlighted in this section, as cancer is the second leading cause of death in the
United States (CDC, 2021). Healthy People 2030, a part of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services,
has created a goal to, “Reduce new cases of cancer and cancer-related illness, disability, and death.” Cancer
screenings are a primary strategy to reach this goal, leading to early detection and improved outcomes.
Houston Health Department                                                       2024 Houston Community Health Assessment
Breast Cancer Screening
A mammogram is an x-ray of the breast that can be used to detect changes in the breast such as tumors and
calcifications. The test may be done for screening or for diagnostic purposes. Mammograms may also be
used to evaluate known cases of breast cancer. A positive screening mammogram leads to further testing to
determine if cancer is present. Although mammograms do not detect all cases of breast cancer, they have
been shown to increase early detection, thus reducing mortality.
The Health People 2030 goal for female breast cancer screenings stands at 80.5%. This is based on women
aged 50-74 years old receiving a breast cancer screening via mammogram in the past two years. The baseline
was set in 2019 when 76.2% of U.S. females aged 50 to 74 years received a breast cancer screening. The most
recent data was 75.6% in 2021 (Healthy People 2030, 2021).
Data Source: Texas Cancer Registry (2019)
The Texas Cancer Registry (2022a) reported the state’s total rate of breast cancer screening in 2018 was 76.6%,
below the national average of 78.3%. Screening rates in Texas varied by race and ethnicity. Black women were
most likely to have had breast cancer screening, followed by White women, Non-Hispanic Other/Multi-Racial
women, and Hispanic women.
Cervical Cancer Screening
Cervical cancer forms in tissues of the cervix (the organ that connects the uterus and vagina) and is slow-
growing. Cervical cancer that is detected early is one of the most successfully treatable cancers. Cervical cancer
is detected by Pap test screenings and is most often caused by human papillomavirus (HPV), which is a type of
infection transmitted through sexual contact.
The target for cervical cancer screenings set by Healthy People 2030 is 84.3%. This is based on data from
women who underwent a Pap test for cervical cancer screening in the previous three years, ranging in age
from 21 to 65.
Cervical screening rates in 2020 were 78.1% for Houston women, compared to 79.5% of Harris County women
and 82.8% of U.S. women (Houston State of Health Data Portal, 2024). These measures show the percentages
of women aged 21-65 who have had a cervical cancer screening test. This is a Pap test every 3 years for women
21-29; and a Pap test every 3 years or an HPV test and Pap test every 5 years for women 30-65.
The following charts illustrate that in Texas, in 2012-2016, women with more education were more likely to
be screened for cervical cancer, as were women with higher incomes (Texas Cancer Registry, 2029). These
disparities may reflect issues such as differences in access to health care, varying levels of health literacy, and
cultural barriers.

Colorectal Cancer ScreeningColorectal Cancer Screening
According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), colorectal cancer, or cancer of the colon
or rectum, is one of the most commonly diagnosed cancers in the United States, and is the third leading cause
of cancer deaths in the U.S. The CDC estimated that if all adults aged 50 or older had regular screening tests
for colon cancer, as many as 60% of the deaths from colorectal cancer could be prevented. The US Preventive
Service Task Force recommends screening begin at age 45 and continue until age 75; however, testing may
need to begin earlier or more frequent if indicated by one’s medical history (Houston State of Health Data
Portal, 2024).
Colon cancer screening rates in 2018 were 53.9% in Houston, 55.4% in Harris County and 66.4% in the U.S. This
indicator shows the percentage of respondents aged 50-75 who have had either a fecal occult blood test in
the past year, a sigmoidoscopy in the past five years AND a fecal occult blood test in the past three years, or a
colonoscopy in the past ten years (Houston State of Health Data Portal, 2024).
The target for colorectal cancer screening set by Healthy People 2030 is 68.3% of those who are recommended
for screening. In 2021, 58.7% of adults in the U.S. aged 45 to 75 years received a colorectal cancer screening
based on the most recent guidelines (Healthy People 2030, 2021).
According to the Texas Cancer Registry (2022b), in 2020, women (69.4%) were more likely to have had colorectal
cancer screening compared to men (64.1%), and those who were insured (72.7%) were more likely to be screened
compared to those without insurance (32.1%).
Data Source: Texas Cancer Registry (2022b)

Prostate Cancer Screening
The prostate is a gland found only in males,
and is located below the bladder and in
front of the rectum. Prostate cancer is a
leading cause of cancer death among
men in the United States. According to the
American Cancer Society, about 1 in 8 men
will be diagnosed with prostate cancer.
About 1 in 41 will die from prostate cancer.
The two greatest risk factors for prostate
cancer are age and race, with men over the
age of 65 and men who are non-Hispanic
Black possessing the highest incidence
rates of prostate cancer in the U.S. (Houston    Data Source: Houston State of Health Data Portal
State of Health Data Portal, 2024.)
The United States Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) currently advises that men between the ages of 55
and 69 discuss the potential advantages and disadvantages of prostate cancer screening with their healthcare
provider before deciding whether to have a prostate-specific antigen (PSA) test. Men aged 70 and older are
not advised to get routine screening.
Lung Cancer Screening
According to the American Lung Association, more people die from lung cancer annually than any other
type of cancer, exceeding the total deaths caused by breast cancer, colorectal cancer, and prostate cancer
combined. The greatest risk factor for lung cancer is duration and quantity of smoking. While the mortality
rate due to lung cancer among men has reached a plateau, the mortality rate due to lung cancer among
women continues to increase. African Americans have the highest risk of developing lung cancer (Houston
State of Health Data Portal, 2024).
The target for increasing lung cancer screenings under Healthy People 2030 is 7.5%. The baseline in 2015
was 4.5% of adults nationwide who had the recommended screening (Healthy People 2030, 2021). The most
common screening method is low-dose computed tomography (LDCT), a kind of X-ray that produces exact
images of the lungs. LDCT screening is suggested for persons who have a high risk of developing lung cancer
as evidenced by meeting all of the following criteria:
• Are ages 50 to 80 years,
• Have a 20 pack-year smoking history, and
• Currently smoke cigarettes or quit in the past 15 years (U.S. Preventive Task Force, 2021).
Screening is usually done annually.
Public Health Actions:
• Provide education to the public through outreach programs to promote cancer screening.
• Partner with community-based organizations to expand awareness of the importance of screenings
and how to access them. Include educational materials that are culturally relevant.
• Support initiatives to expand healthcare access to affordable cancer screenings for low-income or
uninsured residents.

Morbidity
Diabetes
Diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disease characterized by persistent hyperglycemia (high blood sugar). This
disease can have a harmful effect on most of the organ systems in the human body; it is a frequent cause of
end-stage renal disease, non-traumatic lower-extremity amputation, and a leading cause of blindness among
working age adults. Persons with diabetes are also at increased risk for ischemic heart disease, neuropathy,
and stroke. Diabetes disproportionately affects minority populations and the elderly, and its incidence is
likely to increase as minority populations grow and the U.S. population ages (Houston State of Health Data
Portal, 2024).
In 2021, 38.4 million or 11.6% of the U.S. population was estimated to have diabetes. Of these, 29.7 million
had been diagnosed. Another 8.7 million were undiagnosed and unaware of their disease. In the same year,
diabetes was the 8th leading cause of death in the U.S. with 103,294 deaths (National Diabetes Statistics
Report, 2024). In 2020, diabetes led to 7,916 deaths in Texas and 1,012 deaths in Harris County (Texas Health
Data Dashboard, 2024).
In 2021, the percentage of residents diagnosed by a medical professional was higher in Houston compared
to Harris County, Texas, or the U.S. In Harris County, in 2021, more males (10.2%) had been diagnosed with
diabetes compared to women (6.7%). Blacks had the highest rate of deaths from diabetes (36.6 per 100,000)
followed by Hispanics (24.1), Whites (18.4), and Asian/Pacific Islanders (14.6). Older adults are more likely to be
diagnosed with diabetes (Houston
State of Health Data Portal, 2024).

Cancer is a disease caused by the uncontrollable
growth of abnormal cells that in some cases
metastasize or spread to other tissues.
Cancerous cells can involve any tissue in the
body and have different forms in each tissue;
therefore, there are over 100 different types
of cancer.
Cancer has been one of the top two leading
causes of death for more than 75 years in the
U.S. Deaths from cancer have decreased over
the past three decades, reflecting factors such
as the decrease in cigarette smoking and
increased use of cancer screening tests. Age-
adjusted cancer deaths per 100,000 population
decreased from 173.5 in 2009 to 155.8 in 2016,
and then decreased at a faster rate to 146.2 in
2019. A total of 599,601 people died of cancer

cases per 100,000, making Texas the state with
the 12th lowest incidence in the country. The
Texas Department of State Health Services
projected that 137,308 new cancer diagnosis
would be made for 2022 in Texas.

Children and Adolescents
Obesity is a serious health concern for children and adolescents in Houston and Harris County. Obese and
overweight youth are more likely to have risk factors associated with cardiovascular diseases, such as high
blood pressure, high cholesterol, and type 2 diabetes, and these conditions are likely to be worse when they
become adults. Childhood obesity in impacted by dietary patterns, physical inactivity, genetics, medications,
and the physical and social environment. (Houston State of Health Data Portal).
The following charts depict that 38.6% of Houston high school students were overweight or obese, compared
to Texas (39.3%) and the U.S. (32.3%) in 2021. Among high school students, Hispanics (43.1%) had the highest
rate of over weight and obesity combined, followed by Black students (37.4%). Obesity rates were: Houston
teens (21.2%), Texas (22.1%), and the U.S. (16.3%). The Healthy People 2030 goal is to reduce childhood and
teenage obesity rates to 15.5%.
Young children between 2 and 4 years are also at risk of obesity. According to the CDC, the prevalence of
obesity in 2020 was higher among young children who were Hispanic (17.4%) and American Indian or Alaska
Native (18.4%) than among those who were White non-Hispanic (12.3%), Black non-Hispanic (11.9%), or Asian
or Pacific Islander (10.7%). The Healthy People 2030 national target is to reduce childhood obesity to 15%.
Public Health Actions:
• Partner with community-based organizations to educate the public about the health consequences
of obesity and how to modify food and lifestyle to combat weight gain.
• Work to change policies, systems, and environments so they support access to healthy food and
opportunities for physical activity.
• Partner with other sectors, such as urban planning, to identify ways to develop projects and strategies
to encourage physical activity and reduce overweight.
• Collaborate with schools and groups such as the School Health Advisory Councils to identify ways to
help decrease obesity among children and adolescents.

STD and HIV
STD
Sexually transmitted diseases (STD) are infections that can spread via sexual contact. Individuals have
increased risk for STD infections if they have unprotected sex (vaginal, anal and/or oral) and/or multiple sex
partners. Those at risk for acquiring a STD should get tested and receive treatment if found to be positive.
Otherwise, untreated STDs can cause long-term health consequences, especially among women and infants
(National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases, 2024). Additionally, people with an STD are more likely
to acquire HIV or transmit it to others. In the U.S., those with STDs are often co-infected with HIV. STDs increase
susceptibility to HIV infection due to the presence of sores or inflammation (CDC, 2022b).
In March 2020, STD program resources shifted to curbing the spread of Covid-19 (CDC, 2022c). This led to
decreased STD screening and testing, which resulted in drastically fewer reported STD cases at the beginning
of the pandemic. The CDC states that STDs are still a major public health concern. Even in the face of a
pandemic, there were 2.4 million reported cases of chlamydia, gonorrhea and syphilis (CDC, 2022d). Overall,
Houston/Harris County had higher rates per 100,000 population of new chlamydia, gonorrhea, and syphilis
diagnoses than both Texas and the U.S. in 2020 (THISIS; CDC, 2022e). Chlamydia, gonorrhea and syphilis are
three of the STDs that must be reported to local or regional Health Departments.
Chlamydia
Chlamydia is the most commonly reported bacterial STD in the U.S. Many people infected with chlamydia
are asymptomatic. If left untreated, health consequences such as pelvic inflammatory disease, infertility, or
ectopic pregnancy can occur. During childbirth, a mother can give chlamydia to her baby, causing eye and
lung infections (CDC, 2024e).

HIV/AIDS
Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (AIDS) was first reported in the United States in 1981 and has since
become pandemic. AIDS is caused by the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV), a virus that attacks cells of
the immune system and destroys the body’s ability to fight off infection.
At the beginning of the epidemic, people died within about 10 years of HIV infection. In 1996, the introduction
of highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART), commonly known as triple cocktail, significantly slowed the
progression of HIV to AIDS and AIDS to death.
HIV infection is most often spread by direct contact with blood, semen, or vaginal fluid during unprotected
sex with a partner living with HIV who had an unsuppressed viral load. It is also spread among persons who
inject substances (drugs, insulin, hormones, etc.) and share needles with a person living with HIV and an
unsuppressed viral load. In addition, any individual able to become pregnant can transmit HIV to their child
during pregnancy, delivery, or through breastfeeding if they are not virally suppressed.
The CDC estimates that in 2020 more than 1,189,700 persons were living with HIV in the U.S. and 13% did not
know their HIV status (CDC, 2022f ). As of 2020, approximately 28,246 people in the Houston/ Harris County
area were living with HIV/AIDS, a rate of 592 per 100,000 population. Of those living with HIV/AIDS, 48% were
Black, 31% were Hispanic, 16% were White, and 76% were assigned male at birth. The following chart shows
the number of new HIV/AIDS diagnoses (red line) and deaths (green line) since HIV cases began in 1981. Over
HIV/AIDS Diagnoses, Deaths, and People Living with Diagnosed HIV or AIDS, Houston/Harris County Area
time, with new treatments, fewer new cases and deaths were reported, and numbers of people living with
AIDS (PLWA) and people living with HIV (PLWH) increased (blue bars).
Reported new HIV cases among adults, regardless of AIDS status, in Houston/Harris County have decreased in
the past five years. However, the largest decrease, from 1,234 in 2019 to 934 in 2020, is likely due to disruptions
to HIV prevention and care services within Houston/Harris County due to the COVID-19 pandemic. The CDC
estimates that 13% of individuals in the United States are living with undiagnosed HIV/AIDS.

Vaccine Preventable Diseases (VPD)
Immunizations protect individuals and the public from communicable diseases caused by both viruses and
bacteria every day. Vaccine-preventable diseases (VPD) such as measles, mumps, rubella, and tetanus have
been either reduced significantly or eradicated with aid from childhood and adult vaccination efforts, with
added contributions from herd immunity. In recent years, however, the cases of mumps have surged. The more
common conditions, including whooping cough (pertussis) and chickenpox (varicella) are still present in the
U.S., warranting continued use of recommended vaccines.
Vaccine-preventable diseases such as diphtheria, polio, and other severe conditions are now rare in the United
States but are still risk factors based on international travel and trade.

Hepatitis
Viral hepatitis is an infection of the liver caused by hepatitis A virus, hepatitis B virus, or hepatitis C virus.
Hepatitis A and hepatitis B are vaccine-preventable and hepatitis C can be cured. Though all three types of
viral hepatitis can cause similar symptoms, each virus is spread in different ways.
According to CDC, hepatitis, or inflammation of the liver, can also be caused by heavy alcohol consumption,
toxins, some medications, and specific medical conditions. The liver is a vital organ that processes nutrients,
filters the blood, and fights infections; when the liver is inflamed or damaged, its function can be impacted. If
untreated, hepatitis can cause short-term illness or long-term, chronic infection that can lead to serious health
issues.

Coronary Heart Disease
Heart disease is an overarching phrase for several conditions that affect the heart’s structure and function.
Coronary heart disease is a heart disease that emanates from coronary artery dysfunction leading to insufficient
supply of oxygen-rich blood to the heart.
About 18.2 million American adults have coronary
artery disease, making it the most common type of
heart disease in the U.S, according to the Centers for
Disease Control and Prevention. Heart disease in general
is consistently the leading cause of death in the U.S. with
estimated 695,547 deaths in 2021.
Similarly, it accounted for 50,281 deaths in Texas in 2020,
making it the number one cause of death in the state.
According to the CDC Wonder, a death certificate-based
data resource, the Harris County age-adjusted death
rate for 2018 - 2020 was 86.6 deaths/100,000 population
which is lower than the estimates for the state of Texas
(93.7 deaths/100,000 population), as well as that for the
nation (90.2 deaths/100,000 population).
The rate of coronary events, also known as heart attacks,
among adults in Houston and Harris County was
relatively stable between years 2018 and 2021. These
rates are consistently lower than those for the state of
Texas and the U.S.
Harris County death rates (age adjusted per 100,000
population) due to coronary artery disease in 2018-2020, were highest among the Black/African American
population, followed by White, Hispanic, and Asian/Pacific Islander.
In Harris County, in 2020, males (57.9 per 100,000) were almost twice as likely to die of a heart attack compared
to females (30.7 per 100,000) (Houston State of Health Data Portal, 2024).
Public Health Actions:
• Partner with community-based organizations to provide public education on risk factors for
cardiovascular disease, including poor diet, low physical activities, and smoking.
• Develop plans and support policies that promote environments with access to healthy foods and
opportunities for physical activity.
• Support healthcare funding policies that increase access to essential medications regardless of
socio-economic status.

Mental Health
Mental health refers to a person’s overall psychological and emotional well-being. It encompasses a range
of factors, including one’s ability to manage emotions, think clearly, make decisions, and form and maintain
relationships. Mental health can also influence a person’s physical health and their ability to function in daily life.
Mental health disorders are conditions that affect a person’s thoughts, feelings, and behaviors in a way that
interferes with their daily life. These disorders can range from mild to severe and can be caused by a variety of
factors, including genetics, environmental factors, and life experiences. Some common mental health disorders
include depression, anxiety disorders, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, and eating disorders. Treatment for
mental health disorders typically involves a combination of therapy, medication, and lifestyle changes.
The prevalence of mental illness in Harris County is similar to that of the rest of the United States. In 2021, an
estimated 1 in 5 adults in Harris County experienced a mental illness, and 1 in 25 adults experienced a serious
mental illness.
The most commonly diagnosed mental health disorders
in Harris County are depression, anxiety disorders, and
substance use disorders. Suicide is also a concern in
the region, with an average of 9.6 suicides per 100,000
residents each year from 2016 to 2018.
Suicide attempts are of concern among youth. In 2021,
12.5% of Houston high school students attempted
suicide, compared to 12.3% in Texas and 10.2% in the
United States. Houston high school females (14.7%)
were more likely to attempt suicide compared to males
Data Source: Houston State of Health Data Portal
(9.2%). Suicide attempts were highest among Black
students, followed by Hispanic/Latino students, White students, and Asian students (Houston State of Health
Data Portal, 2024).
Depression is a common mental health disorder that can
affect people of all ages and backgrounds, including those
living in Houston. According to a survey conducted by the
Houston Health Department in 2018, depression was the
most commonly reported mental health concern among
Harris County residents; approximately 1 in 10 adults in Harris
County reported experiencing depressive symptoms. Anxiety
was the second rated mental health concern; approximately
1 in 12 Harris County adults reported anxiety symptoms.
The overall trend of Harris County residents reporting poor
mental health of five or more days in the past month steadily increased from 14.9% in 2013 to 27.0% in 2021.
Females (30.5%) were more likely to report 5+ days of poor mental health compared to males (23.7%). Blacks
(35.1%) were more likely to report 5+ days of poor mental health compared to Whites (33.4%) and Hispanics
(17.4%) (Houston State of Health Data Portal, 2024).
Access to mental health care can be a challenge for some residents of Houston and Harris County, particularly
for those who are uninsured or underinsured. In 2021, approximately 1 in 3 adults in Harris County did not
have health insurance.
Others may face barriers to seeking care due to negative stigmas about mental health issues. This stigma varies
based on gender or ethnic and cultural beliefs and attitudes (Ahad, Sanchez-Gonzalez & Junquera, 2024).
Houston Health Department                                                                           2024 Houston Community Health Assessment
Public Health Actions:
• Partner with community-based organizations to provide accurate and culturally sensitive information
about mental health disorders to the community to
• help overcome stigma and encourage those with symptoms to seek care.
• Educate the public about options for seeking mental health care.
• Support expansion of mental health services and community resources such as support groups, peer-
to-peer programs, and other community-based services.
• Promote mental health in the workplace through options such as employee assistance programs and
creating a culture that supports mental health and well-being.
Infant and Maternal Health
Maternal and infant health is an indicator for the health and well-being of our future generation. Maternal
health includes aspects such as pregnancy complications, depression and weight gain during pregnancy,
substance abuse, gestational diabetes, and maternal mortality. Infant health includes topics such as preterm
birth, Sudden Infant Death Syndrome, and infant mortality (CDC, 2022h).
Adolescent Pregnancy

and job opportunities (Houston

Preterm Birth
Preterm birth is defined as a baby born before 37 weeks of
pregnancy. Babies born too early, and especially those born before 32 weeks, have higher rates of death and
disability. In 2022, the rate of preterm birth in the U.S. among African-American women (14.6%) was about 50
percent higher than the rate of preterm birth among White (9.4%) or Hispanic (10.1%) women. Preterm birth
is also higher among young mothers under age 20 and older mothers over age 40 (CDC, 2024i).
2024 Houston Community Health Assessment                                                                          Houston Health Department
Preterm birth rates in Harris County have been relatively steady, with little fluctuation since 2009, when the
percentage of preterm births was 13.2%, to 2019, when the percentage was still at 13.2% (Houston State of
Health Data Portal, 2024).
Black women (17.1%) had the highest rates of preterm labor and delivery in Harris County as of 2019, followed
by Hispanics (13.1%), Whites (11.1%), and Others (10.8%). The highest rates of preterm birth were in the oldest
group of 40 and older (21.2%) and the youngest group of 17 and younger (15.9%); other groups were ages
Low Birth Weight
Low birthweight is defined as a baby born weighing less than 5 pounds, 8 ounces. Some babies with low
birthweight are born healthy, even though they’re small. But low birth weight can also cause serious health
problems for babies. These infants may have trouble eating, gaining weight and fighting off infections, as
well as long-term health problems. About 1 in 12 babies (about 8%) in the United States is born with low
birthweight. While the causes of preterm birth and low birthweight may be different in some cases, there is
significant overlap within these populations of infants (March of Dimes, 2021).
In Harris County, the percentage of low-birth-weight infants has remained relatively stable. In 2009, the
percentage was 8.9%; in 2019, the percentage was 9.0% of all births. This can be compared to 8.4% of births
in Texas and 8.3% in the U.S. in 2019 (Texas Health Data, 2024; Martin, 2021).
In 2019, Black women had the highest percent of low weight births in Harris County at 14.8% compared to
Hispanics (7.7%) and Whites (6.9%). The rates of low-birth-weight babies for Black and mothers with race/
ethnicity described as Other exceeded the rate of Harris County overall.
The rate of low weight births was highest among the youngest and oldest mothers. The percentage of low-
birth-weight babies among women 40 years or older was 13.7%, followed by mothers 17 years and younger
at 9.6%. Mothers aged 20-29 years had the lowest percentage at 8.6%.

Infant Mortality
Infant mortality is the death of an infant before their first birthday. The infant mortality rate is the number of
infant deaths for every 1,000 live births. In addition to giving us key information about maternal and infant
health, the infant mortality rate is an important marker of the overall health of a society. The leading causes
of death among infants are birth defects, preterm delivery, low birth weight, Sudden Infant Death Syndrome
(SIDS), injuries, and pregnancy complications (Houston State of Health Data Portal, 2024).
In Harris County, in 2019, the infant mortality rate
was 5.9 per 1,000 live births, compared to 5.5 in Texas
(Texas Health Data Dashboard, n.d.). CDC reported a
rate of 5.6 in the U.S. in 2019. The Healthy People 2030
target is 5.0.
In Texas, in 2019, the leading causes of infant mortality
were listed as congenital malformations, deformations,
and chromosome abnormalities (471 deaths),
followed by disorders related to short gestation and
low birthweight (301 deaths), and newborn affected
by maternal complications of pregnancy (122 deaths)

• Educate women and families about health matters regarding pregnancy and healthy practices during

• Collaborate with community partners to identify barriers to healthy pregnancy and recommended

• Monitor health status by tracking high risk groups such as pregnant adolescents and infants in the first

COVID-19
Overview
COVID-19 (coronavirus disease 2019) is a disease caused by a virus named SARS-CoV-2 and was discovered in
December 2019 in Wuhan, China. It is very contagious and quickly spread around the world. It is part of the
coronavirus family, which include common viruses that cause a variety of diseases from head or chest colds
to more severe (but rarer) diseases like severe acute respiratory syndrome (SARS) and Middle East respiratory
syndrome (MERS).
Like many other respiratory viruses, coronaviruses spread quickly through droplets that are projected out of
the mouth or nose through breathing, coughing, sneezing, or speaking.
Most people with COVID-19 have mild respiratory symptoms that can feel much like a cold, a flu, or pneumonia,
yet may also attack other parts of the body; others have become severely ill. Some people including those
with minor or no symptoms may suffer from post-COVID conditions, often called “long COVID.” Older adults
and people who have certain underlying medical conditions are at increased risk of severe illness from COVID,
causing death in some instances.
As of March 2024, CDC reported 103,801,821 COVID-19 cases and 1,121,512 COVID-19 deaths in the United
States (CDC, 2024j). The following chart shows the weekly count of COVID-19 cases (green bars) and the weekly
rate of COVID-19 deaths per 100,000 population (orange line) from January 29, 2020, until March 22, 2024, in
the U.S. The death rate reached a peak about one year after the pandemic began. Then, after vaccines were
introduced early in 2021, deaths began to fall. Another peak occurred toward the end of 2021, as the Omicron
variant emerged.
Data Source: CDC COVID Data Tracker
Vaccines against COVID-19 have proven to be safe and effective, and teach our immune system to fight the
virus that causes COVID-19. Approximately 239.7 million people or 69.5% of the U.S. population completed
the primary series of the vaccination. About 54.3 million people or 16.2% of the U.S. population have received
an updated Booster dose (CDC, 2024l).
Houston Health Department                                                                                             2024 Houston Community Health Assessment
Trends: Houston/Harris County
Since the discovery of the novel SARS-CoV-2 virus in early 2020, cases slowly began to increase. In early 2021,
cases continued to increase along with the push for and uptake in vaccines, testing, and education. Toward
the end of 2021, a drastic increase in the number of case reports were documented totaling 13,900 cases in
December 2021. During this time, over 4,000 COVID-19 samples were collected resulting with the Omicron
variant the most dominant variant over time. Between 2022 to 2024, cases in Houston/Harris County began to
decline with 33 cases reported in April 2024. The following chart tracks cases in Houston/Harris County from
early 2020 through March of 2024.
Data Source: Harris County / City of Houston COVID-19 Data Hub
As genetic changes to the virus happened over time, the SARS-CoV-2 virus began to form genetic lineages
which have different attributes that affect how fast the virus can spread, the severity of illness it causes, or the
effectiveness of treatments against it. Scientists call the viruses with these changes “variants” which are still
considered SARS-CoV-2 but may act differently (CDC 2024k).
Population Differences in Houston
The elderly were at especially high risk during the COVID-19 pandemic. High transmission of the virus
was observed within Long Term Care Facilities (LTCF) that serve the elderly. Factors contributing to high
transmissibility of the virus in these facilities include the age of the population, the close proximity of other
residents in the setting, and the existence of underlying medical conditions. According to the HHD Outbreak
Management Unit, local LTCSs experienced an increase in cases in December, 2022, due to the Omicron variant
with 473 cases reported, the highest number reported of cases in a week, since the inception.

diseases, such as COVID-19.
• Assure the provision of healthcare when otherwise unavailable by providing immunizations to
low-income persons.
• Mobilize community partnerships and action to identify barriers to eliminating COVID-19.
• Increase participation in community-wide efforts to increase awareness of the benefits of

Mortality
Life Expectancy & Leading Causes of Death in Houston/Harris County Area
Across the United States, life expectancy has declined since 2019 (National Center for Health Statistics.
(2022a). The National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS, 2022b) reports that declines in 2020, and again in
2021, reduced U.S. life expectancy to its lowest level since 1996. In 2020, life expectancy dropped from 78.8
to 77.0 years, followed by a further drop to 76.4 years in 2021. This decline is mirrored at the local level in data
from the Big Cities Health Inventory (BCHI) for Houston (Big Cities Health Inventory Dashboard, n.d.).
At the national level, this decline represents the largest drop in life expectancy over a two-year period since the
1920s (National Center for Health Statistics (2022a). The primary contributing factors were COVID-19 deaths
and drug overdose. According to the NCHS, in the United States:

The Houston Health Department Wants to Hear From You!
The Houston Health Department wants to know what you think are the top health
concerns in your neighborhood. We have developed a short survey to get your feedback.
Your responses are voluntary and will be kept confidential. If you have any questions or
concerns regarding this survey, please email at hhdplanning@houstontx.gov.
1. What zip code do you currently live in? ___________________________________________
2. What are the top three issues that can prevent someone in your community from having a fair
opportunity to be as healthy as possible?
•   Health care
•   Educational opportunities
•   Immigration status (documented/undocumented)
•   Stable source of income
•   Health insurance
•   Poverty in your community
•   Racism
•   Segregation
•   Discrimination due to disability, sexual orientation, gender, etc.
•   Other, Please specify__________

are concerned about?

•   Exposure to toxins
•   Food insecurity
•   Access to food safety
•   Housing including gentrification
•   Exposure to lead poisoning
•   Neighborhood concerns including crime/violence, noise, graffiti
•   Neighborhood segregation (residential)
•   Surface water quality/solid waste
•   Access to transportation
•   Access to water for drinking
•   Other, Please specify__________
4. Using the list below, please choose the top three risk behaviors in your neighborhood?
•   Alcohol and drug use
•   Child abuse and neglect
•   More emergency room visits
